---
output: 
  pdf_document:
    citation_package: natbib
    keep_tex: false
    fig_caption: true
    toc: false
    latex_engine: pdflatex
    template: /Users/serpent/Documents/R Resources/Markdown Temps/svm-latex-ms.tex
bibliography: master.bib
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{pdfpages}
  - \AtBeginDocument{\includepdf[pages=1 scale=1, fitpaper=true]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Dissertation/images/cover/cover.pdf}}
  - \usepackage{array}   
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{siunitx}
  - \usepackage[table]{xcolor}
  - \definecolor{navyblue}{RGB}{80, 80, 80} 
  - \definecolor{darkblue}{RGB}{0, 0, 139}  
  - \usepackage{multirow}
  - \usepackage{hhline}
  - \usepackage{calc}
  - \usepackage{tabularx}
  - \usepackage{fontawesome}
  - \usepackage[para,online,flushleft]{threeparttable}
  - \usepackage{setspace}
  - \setstretch{1.5}
  - \usepackage{helvet}
  - \renewcommand{\familydefault}{\sfdefault}
  - \usepackage{fancyhdr}
  - \usepackage{etoolbox}
  - \setlength{\headheight}{38.68912pt}  
  - \addtolength{\topmargin}{-20pt}  
  - \setlength{\headsep}{20pt} 
  - \AtBeginEnvironment{thebibliography}{\linespread{1.5}\selectfont}
  - \DeclareCaptionFont{navyblue}{\color{navyblue}}  
  - \captionsetup{font={stretch=1.25,small,navyblue,sf}, labelfont={bf,navyblue}, textfont=sf, justification=justified, singlelinecheck=false}
  - \sloppy                  
  - \emergencystretch=3em      
  - \hyphenpenalty=500        
  - \tolerance=1000           
  - \exhyphenpenalty=1000  
  - \usepackage{float}  
  - \pagestyle{fancy}  
  - \fancyhf{} 
  - \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}  
  - \fancyhead[L]{}  
  - \fancyhead[R]{} 
  - \fancyhead[C]{\includegraphics[width=1cm]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Dissertation/images/portico.png}} 
  - \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage}  
  - \usepackage{hyperref} 
  - \usepackage[toc,page]{appendix}
  - \usepackage{titlesec}
  - \usepackage{titlesec}
  - \titleformat{\section}[block]{\normalsize\bfseries}{SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIAL}{1em}{}
  - \renewcommand{\bibsection}{\section*{\MakeUppercase{REFERENCES}}}
biblio-style: apalike
title: "Space Over Time: Functional Traits Reveal Environmental Feedbacks Driving Forest Succession"
abstract: " **Abstract:** Succession is a fundamental driver of tree diversity and closely links to forest productivity, vulnerability, and resilience. Environmental changes are altering successional dynamics, raising the need to better understand succession across large spatial and temporal scales. However, a key challenge lies in unravelling the importance, mechanisms, and predictability of feedback dynamics between trees and their environment. In this study, we partitioned succession into trajectories of eight traits representing key functional clusters in trees, providing the first comprehensive view of trait succession across environments. We used machine learning to model trait trajectories across forest standage, while accounting for the effects of abiotic conditions. We then compared trait trajectories under scenarios of high and low conditions to infer the importance, mechanisms, and predictability of abiotic conditions driving succession. Our results show that temperature and elevation are the dominant predictors of most traits, limiting functional turnover except in traits primarily associated with resource competition. Climate conditions and elevation strongly and deterministically filter functional groups at the outset of succession, but also modulate successional trajectories, driving community divergence across different environments. Temperature-induced divergence may therefore be a previously unrecognized consequence of climate change, underscoring the need to establish relationships between global warming and community divergence to better prepare for successional shifts and to develop adaptive management strategies."
keywords: "Trait Succession, Community Assembly, Machine Learning, Random Forest"
geometry: margin=1in
fontsize: 12pt
endnote: no
linkcolor: darkblue
citecolor: darkblue
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, echo = FALSE)
```

\newpage

# INTRODUCTION

Forests are hotspots of biodiversity and provide vital ecosystem services like carbon storage, regulation of water and nutrient cycles, or prevention of soil degradation [@mori2017]. After a century of significant forest loss [@curtis2018; @kumar2022], deforestation rates have decelerated since the 1990s, while planted forests have increased by over 120 million hectares [@faoGlobalForestResources2020a]. Nevertheless, over 85% of global forests remain threatened according to the IUCN Red List of Ecosystems criteria [@ferrer-paris2019]. One reason is the cumulative effect of land use and, more recently, climate change [@curtis2018] in shifting successional dynamics [@andrewsa2018]. This poses a particular threat to regenerating secondary forests, the dominant forest type globally [@faoGlobalForestResources2020a], which have widely scattered age distributions and developmental stages [@besnard2021]. Therefore, understanding successional pathways is a cornerstone in contemporary ecology [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023] and has been a foundational objective for decades [@chang2019; @christensen2014; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. The relationships between succession and the environment are especially pertinent to forestry and forest conservation, as forest productivity, sensitivity to threats, and the scale of ecosystem services they provide vary across succession [@andrewsa2018; @haeussler2021; @malhiAllocationEcosystemNet2011; @sanchez-pinillos2019]. Therefore, successional pathways guide conservation prioritization [@christensen2014; @odum1969] and inform how ecosystem services can be enhanced, vulnerabilities mitigated, and the resilience of forests promoted in the face of increasing land use pressures and climate change [@christensen2014; @mori2017; @odum1969].\

\noindent In secondary forests, succession describes how communities assemble and change over time following a discrete disturbance event [@chang2019; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. Most successional theories recognize a sequential turnover in tree species composition and functional groups over time, often separating *early*, *intermediate*, and *late* stages of succession [@poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. Transitions between stages are commonly understood in the context of community assembly and species co-existence theories [@chang2016; @hillerislambers2012], which suggest that transitions are driven by feedback dynamics between trees and their biotic or abiotic environments [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024]. For instance, low competition and favourable light conditions during early succession generally promote high species diversity and functional traits oriented towards resource acquisition [@skutch1929; @swanson2011]. Increased competition during intermediate succession often leads to stem exclusion, i.e., the establishment of taller trees and canopy closure [@poorterSuccessionalTheories2023; @vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024]. This limits resource availability in late-stage succession, ultimately stabilizing a complex community structure characterized by traits that emphasize resource conservation, robustness, and longevity [@lebrija-trejos2010a; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023; @sanchez-pinillos2019].\

\noindent Since feedback mechanisms should create predictable trajectories, successional pathways have long been considered orderly, directional, and determined by mostly autogenic factors [@christensen2014; @odum1969]. Ergo, successional pathways were typically inferred from chronosequences, i.e., space-for-time substitutions [@johnson2008a]. However, research of the past 50 years observed a substantial variability in succession [@christensen2014] across environments and within similar stands [@chazdon2006; @feldpausch2007a; @johnson2008a; @vandermeer2004], challenging the robustness of chronosequences for drawing successional inferences [@chang2016; @christensen2014; @norden2015]. Looking forward, the creation of novel assemblages by climate change [@ordonez2016] is likely to introduce further variability in successional patterns when species adapt to changing conditions [@vantiel2024].\

\noindent Identifying unifying trends in succession across temporal and spatial scales is therefore crucial for holistically understanding successional pathways and continuously forecasting them under novel conditions [@poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. However, traditional approaches to quantify trends through taxonomy face several limitations. First, feedback dynamics are most often related to traits or phylogeny [@levinsEvolutionChangingEnvironments2020; @kraft2010]. Second, due to the long timescales involved in succession, most taxonomic studies rely on correlative approaches that often depend on simulations or null models to infer underlying mechanisms. These approaches are contentious, as they can easily conflate different ecological processes, leading to potential misinterpretations [@connor1979]. Third, taxonomic approaches struggle in novel environments where no-analog communities may emerge [@williamsProjectedDistributionsNovel2007]. Functional traits offer a way forward because they provide a mechanistic perspective on species turnover and stage transitions while escaping taxonomic constraints [@kraft2015; @kraft2015a]. By partitioning succession into functional trajectories, it becomes possible to assess the importance, mechanisms, and determinism of feedback dynamics in community assembly along distinct axes in trait space [@douma2012].\

\noindent The relative importance of environment-tree feedbacks, i.e., abiotic conditions governing tree assembly [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024; @kraft2015a], may for example be inferred from a trait's signal throughout succession [@grime1988; @ackerly2007]. If conditions strongly selects for trait expressions that ensure success in a given environment, they inhibit functional turnover over time [@kraft2015a]. Selected traits are then static contributors to a tree's survival chance throughout succession, reflecting adaptation to environmental constraints, with the successional stage of the forest having little impact on survival. Conversely, a high successional signal indicates a high functional turnover [@kraft2010], and trait trajectories to be predominantly governed by changing interactions between co-occurring species [@chesson2000; @kunstler2016; @mayfield2010; @kraft2010]. These traits are dynamic contributors to a tree's survival chances, and certain expressions can be more or less favourable at different successional stages. \

\noindent Moreover, the underlying mechanisms of environment-tree feedbacks may be inferred from differences between trait trajectories across environments [@boukili2017; @kraft2015a; @lebrija-trejos2010]. For example, abiotic conditions could either act as environmental filters, selecting for trait expressions at the outset of succession and thereby determine which trees are able to establish and persist initially [@kraft2015a; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023]. They could also modulate successional pathways of traits across time if trajectories differ with abiotic conditions, leading to divergence or convergence in community assembly between environments as succession progresses [@kraft2015a; @poorterSuccessionalTheories2023; @douma2012]. Both mechanisms may operate independently or synergistically, with the abiotic conditions both filtering the outset and continuously modulating successional trajectories.\

\noindent Additionally, the determinism of environment-tree feedbacks may be inferred from their predictability, offering an additional axis to identify traits driving successional variability across environments [@arroyo-rodriguezLandscapescaleForestCover2023; @norden2015]. A trait's predictability may generally be high but can vary across environments with different conditions due to either filtering, where similar trajectories have different starting points, or modulation, where trajectories diverge over time [@douma2012; @kraft2015a]. Less predictable traits may still respond to abiotic conditions but follow more stochastic paths, suggesting a greater role for allogenic factors or neutral processes [@hubbellUnifiedNeutralTheory2011; @boukili2017]. Integrating the predictability of trait feedbacks with their underlying mechanisms offers insights into how novel communities may emerge under environmental change and the extent to which these communities may assemble deterministically [@guoClimateWarmingLeads2018].\

\noindent Understanding the relative importance, mechanisms, and predictability of environmental feedback dynamics driving trait trajectories could be a step towards a unified theory of succession [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024], ultimately enhancing management strategies for forests facing the impacts of climate change [@andrewsa2018; @vantiel2024]. However, a lack of sufficiently large, homogeneous datasets across traits, space, and time has so far limited our understanding of trait succession and the confounding effects of environmental feedback dynamics [@kraft2015a; @kraft2015; @lohbeck2014a; @norden2015; @chang2019].\

\noindent Here, we provide the first comprehensive view of trait succession across a wide temporal and biogeographic scale, aiming to answer three fundamental questions [@vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024]: (1) What are the dominant environmental feedback dynamics for determining successional trait trajectories? (2) What are the mechanisms of abiotic feedback dynamics with respect to filtering and modulating traits? (3) How does the individual sensitivity of traits to abiotic feedback mechanisms induce successional variability across environments?\

\noindent To archive this, we focused on eight traits representing functional cluster and key aspects of successional strategies [@maynard2022]: Bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, seed dry mass, shade tolerance, specific leaf area, tree height, and wood density. We used machine learning to model the expression of these traits as a function of standage and environmental predictors, utilizing data from more than 55 000 forest plots across the United States. We then predicted the trajectory of traits through succession and explored scenarios of both high and low abiotic conditions.\

\noindent We hypothesize (1) abiotic feedback mechanisms to have a higher relative importance [@kraft2015a]; (2) filtering to be the dominant mechanism of abiotic feedback dynamics [@bouchard2024; @boukili2017], and (3) variability across environments to be induced by strong abiotic modulation (and not filtering) of traits linked to competition for resources, such as those governing light acquisition [@poorter2008; @kunstler2016]. By answering these questions, we aim to better understand how the environment drives community assembly through the lens of traits, contributing towards a unified theory of succession. \newpage

# MATERIAL AND METHODS

## **Data collection and preparation**

### Forest Data

We retrieved tree occurrence data from the US Forest Inventory Analysis [@grayForestInventoryAnalysis2012], a long-term dataset spanning 13 biomes since the 1930s. We initially filtered 37 984 uniformly sampled plots across the United States (excluding Hawaii) with known standage, inventory year, and latitude-longitude coordinates. In the FIA, standage is approximated by the diameter and height of the most abundant age class [@grayForestInventoryAnalysis2012], a common proxy for successional stages. To focus on early to late-stage succession (1–150 years) [@wilfahrt2014; @oliverForestStandDynamics1996], we excluded plots in the upper 10% quantile of standage to avoid the influence of extremely old plots. Privately owned plots and those actively managed for forestry were also excluded. Up to three repeated measures of the same plots were included if taken at least 10 years apart. Georeferenced plots were then grouped into biomes using RESOLVE biome codes (see supplementary material \ref{tab:S3}), and plots from biomes with fewer than 100 observations were excluded, resulting in 55 985 plots for analysis (Fig. \ref{fig:fig1}).

### Tree Traits

To quantify functional trait expression at a given standage, we integrated species-level trait data. Tree height was retrieved from the FIA data where available, while species-specific estimates of traits capturing functionalities of the stem (bark thickness, wood density), leaf economics (specific leaf area, leaf nitrogen), water transportation (conduit diameter), growth and light acquisition (tree height, shade tolerance), and reproduction (seed dry mass) were sourced from a cleaned, phylogenetically imputed subset of the TRY database (v. 5.0) [@kattge2020a] available from @maynard2022. Additionally, we included an index of species-specific shade tolerance from @rueda2018 after imputing missing values following a phylogenetic nearest-neighbour lookup using *phylomaker* in R [@jin2019] (see supplementary material \ref{tab:S1} for trait descriptives).

```{=tex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=3.76in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/fig1.png}
\caption{Heatmap showing the number of plots included in the analysis across the United States (main map showing the US mainland, bottom left map showing Alaska). Note that polygons may extend into the ocean due to the density smoothing, but no plots where actually measured in the ocean.}
\label{fig:fig1}
\end{figure}
```
\noindent Trait values were log-transformed and scaled around zero to stabilize variance and allow for unitless comparison across traits. We summarized the trait expression per species per plot as the weighted plot mean of trait values across trees belonging to one species, weighted by the species-specific basal area scaled to the number of trees of one species per acre. Scaled basal area was calculated as:

$$
\text{basal area (scaled)} = \left[\pi \times \left(\frac{\text{diameter}}{2}\right)^2\right] \times \frac{n_{\text{tree}}}{\text{acre}},
$$

where $n_{\text{tree}}$ is the number of trees of a given species found in a plot.\

\noindent Finally, we calculated the trait expression per plot as the weighted mean of species-per-plot trait values, weighted by the sum of scaled basal areas per species.

### Environmental Features

Environmental predictors were selected to capture key abiotic conditions affecting tree distribution and productivity [@maynard2022] and included climate, elevation, and soil characteristic retrieved from a global composite [@vandenhoogen2019]. Climate variables originated from the CHELSEA data set [@karger2017] and included annual mean temperature, minimum, maximum, and mean temperatures of the coldest and warmest months, annual precipitation, and precipitation of the wettest, driest, warmest, and coldest quarters. Soil characteristics were retrieved from the SoilGrid250 layer [@hengl2017] and included sand content, water capacity, and pH at 15cm and 60cm depths. Elevation data was obtained from @amatulli2018. The georeferenced data were resampled in a unified pixel grid at a 30 arcsec resolution (EPSG:4326 (WGS84) at ca. 1 km$^2$) and values were extracted for every latitude and longitude coordinate of our forest plots [@vandenhoogen2019] by averaging the mean values.\

\noindent To reduce high collinearity among environmental variables and to avoid unrealistic scenarios in trait predictions, we performed a principal component analysis (PCA) to identify underlying axes across temperature, precipitation, and soil variables. We excluded soil pH from the PCA due to its variability in both sandy and water-retentive soils, and elevation to directly control for its effect and simplify the interpretation of the principal components. We fitted the PCA model after testing suitability using the KMO criterion and Bartlett's test of sphericity, identified three principal components with eigenvalues greater than one (see supplementary supplementary material \ref{fig:S4}a), and rotated their loadings using varimax rotation for clearer interpretation. The first component, accounting for 42.1% of the variance, was strongly positively related to temperature variables. The second component, accounting for 23.9% of the variance, was negatively related to sand content and positively related to water capacity. The third component, accounting for 17.8% of the variance, was positively related to precipitation variables (see \ref{fig:S4} b.--d. for details; and \ref{tab:S2} for feature descriptives).

## Data Analysis

We used random forest regression in the *ranger* package [@wright2017] in R to model trait expression as a function of standage and environmental predictors within a machine learning framework. We also included binary variables representing biome association of plots to account for biogeographic differences. This approach allowed us to accurately assess predictability and capture potential non-linearity in trait trajectories. Random forest regression is an ensemble learning method that builds multiple decision trees and averages their predictions to estimate trait values more accurately and robustly. The algorithm relies on a specified set of hyperparameters that, among others, determine splitting rules, variable selection, and stopping criteria for each tree. To ensure model reliability, we split the data into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets, and performed hyperparameter tuning on each trait model using cross-validation across a grid of hyperparameters (see supplementary material \ref{fig:S5}) in the *caret* package [@kuhn2008] to optimize model performance. We then selected the set of hyperparameters yielding the lowest prediction error and fitted trait models using the calibrated hyperparameters and the full dataset.\

\noindent To understand the directionality and relative importance of features, we computed shapley values from the trait models using 100 simulations with the fastshap package [@greenfastshap2024]. Shapley values use a game theory based approach to measure each variable's impact on the outcome for every data point by considering the influence of all features in the model, similarly to partial regression [@marcilio2020; @shapley1997]. For every model, we then ranked the features by their overall importance, calculated as the sum of their absolute shapley values (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a) and calculated the mean rank per feature. \

\noindent We then examined relationships between traits and environmental features using partial dependence, which calculates the marginal effect of a variable on the predicted outcome, allowing us to understand how changes in that feature influence model predictions while averaging out the effects of other features [@friedmanGreedyFunctionApproximation2001]. We fitted partial dependence curves using the *pdp* package [@greenwellPdpPackageConstructing2017] for our global trait models and calculated the successional signal of traits as the difference between the trait expression at the final predicted stand age (150 years) and the curve’s intercept (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b, note that the visualized curves are smoothed by fitting a Generalized Additive Model with cubic spline smoothing). We then compared signals between traits, assuming low signals indicated high importance of abiotic feedbacks, while high signals suggested strong biotic feedbacks. \

\noindent Next, we stratified our data based on the upper and lower 25% quantiles of environmental features to create “high” and “low” scenario subsets, effectively capturing more extreme variations in features while minimizing the influence of outliers. We then used the stratified data to fit novel random forest models for all traits in each scenario and calculated partial dependence curves for each trait in every scenario (supplementary material \ref{fig:S6}). This approach offered several advantages over the common method of predicting traits across an environmental grid using global models. First, it restricted predictions to observed feature combinations, avoiding extrapolation into unobserved conditions. Second, it implicitly captured non-linear patterns and interactions between environmental features and biomes, which were evident from significant two-way ANOVA tests (all *p* \< 0.05). Third, it allowed us to assess model sensitivity and quantify predictive uncertainty across different environmental conditions. Finally, this method enabled us to capture potential heterogeneous effects of environmental features on trait outcomes, such as a feature having a strong positive effect on the trait in the lower quantile but a weaker or negative effect in the upper quantile.\

\noindent Using the stratified partial dependence data, we retrieved the predicted intercept and calculated the successional signal of traits for each feature scenario after visually confirming that the trajectories were sufficiently linear to make this value meaningful (supplementary material \ref{fig:S6}). We then calculated differences in intercepts and successional signals between feature scenarios, both averaged across traits (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}a) and for individual traits (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b). This enabled us to assess the magnitude of differences at the intercept (indicative of abiotic filtering at succession onset) and the signal (indicative of abiotic modulation throughout succession) between high and low feature scenarios. Next, we positioned each trait in a space defined by abiotic filtering and modulation, represented by the mean intercept difference and mean signal difference across feature scenarios (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}c), allowing us to identify the dominant mechanisms driving successional differences across environments for individual traits.\

\noindent To evaluate the abiotic predictability (and thus determinism) versus variability of trait trajectories across environments, we retrieved the $\textit{R}^2$ values of each model. Note that these $\textit{R}^2$ values include standage as a predictive variable, as standage was used to predict trait expressions; therefore, they do not solely reflect the predictive power of abiotic features. We also fitted random forest models for each trait across all feature scenarios excluding standage and recorded their respective $\textit{R}^2$ values to quantify the influence of standage on our approximation of abiotic predictability (see supplementary material \ref{fig:S9}).Finally, we compared the magnitude of abiotic filtering and modulation for individual traits with the mean $\textit{R}^2$ of the respective models, positioning traits within a space defined by the importance of abiotic filtering (x axis in Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a) or modulation (x axis in Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}b) and their predictability (y axis in \ref{fig:fig4}). This allowed us to determine which mechanisms contributed to environmental variability in specific traits and assess their predictability. All analyses were done in R v.4.3.2. [@rcore2023]. \newpage

# RESULTS

## **Importance of environmental features**

The 55 841 forest plots used in this study had standages ranging from 1--150 years at a mean of 75.71 $\pm$ 33.2 years ($\pm$ standard deviation) and spanned 8 different biomes. In our global models, environmental features along with biome association and standage explained a substantial proportion of variance across most traits (mean $\textit{R}^2$ = 0.65 $\pm$ 0.04), with only the model for tree height having an $\textit{R}^2$ below 0.6 (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a.).

### Abiotic tree-environment feedbacks

\noindent Across all trait models, temperature (mean rank 1.63 $\pm$ 0.38 ($\pm$ standard error), Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a) and elevation (mean rank 2.63 $\pm$ 0.38, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a) emerged as the most influential features, consistently ranking among the top three predictors for all traits except shade tolerance. Temperature was the primary factor for five traits (wood density, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, specific leaf area, seed dry mass), with higher temperatures increasing trait expressions and lower temperatures having the opposite effect (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a). Cold temperatures particularly reduced the expression of leaf-related traits and conduit diameter, while bark thickness and seed dry mass showed strong increases at higher temperatures (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a). Elevation exhibited similar effects, with stem-related traits showing a pronounced negative response to lower elevations, while conduit diameter strongly increased at higher elevations (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a). Precipitation (mean rank 3.75 $\pm$ 0.53, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a) and pH (mean rank 4.25 $\pm$ 0.41, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a) also ranked highly, consistently placing in the top three for three traits and in the top five for seven. Precipitation was particularly important for determining shade tolerance, generally increasing trait expressions at higher values and decreasing them at lower values (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a). We observed similar trend for pH, which notably decreased tree height (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a). The soil’s capacity to retain water versus its sandiness showed lower importance (mean rank 6.375 $\pm$ 0.18, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a) and lacked strong directionality for most traits (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a).


```{=tex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=6.9in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/fig2.png}
\caption[Shortened Caption]{a. Shapley values of best fitting trait models (left sub-plots) indicating relative feature importance and directionality. Features are arranged descendingly by their overall importance (right sub-plots) and include biome association (B-BF = Boreal Forests or Taiga, B-FG = Flooded Grasslands, B-MW = Mediterranean Woodlands, B-TB = Temperate Broadleaf Forest, B-TC = Temperate Coniferous Forest, B-TG = Temperate Grasslands, B-TU = Tundra, B-XS = Xeric Shrublands), elevation (ELEV), pH of the soil (pH), precipitation (PRCP (PC)), soil water retention (SOIL (PC)), standage (STAGE), and temperature (TEMP (PC). Negative Shapley values indicate}
\label{fig:fig2}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]\ContinuedFloat
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption[]{a negative effect of features on the respective trait expression, while positive Shapley values indicate a positive effect. High and low feature values are represented by the color gradient. b. Partial dependence curves of traits across standage. The signal (see plot titles) represents the difference between late (150 years) and early succession (0 years) on a log-scale.}
\end{figure}
```
\noindent Biome association had little impact on trait expression (mean ranks: Coniferous Forest = 5.5 $\pm$ 0.9, Temperate Broadleaf Forest = 6.8 $\pm$ 0.5, Temperate Grassland = 9 $\pm$ 0, Boreal Forest or Taiga = 10.5 $\pm$ 0.27, Xeric Shrublands = 10.8 $\pm$ 0.16, Mediterranean Woodlands = 12.63 $\pm$ 0.46, Tundra = 12.3 $\pm$ 0.32, Flooded Grasslands = 13.5 $\pm$ 0.19, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a), except for bark thickness, where location in a temperate coniferous forest was as important a predictor as temperature (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a).

### Biotic tree-environment feedbacks

\noindent Standage, representing biotic feedback dynamics causing turnover across succession, had a relatively low importance for most traits (mean rank 5.5 $\pm$ 1.0), except for shade tolerance and tree height (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a). Standage was the most important feature for determining tree height, with taller trees associated with increased standage (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a). Similarly, standage increased shade tolerance and contributed substantially towards explaining its expression (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a).

### Relative importance of feedbacks

Overall, abiotic features played a dominant role in explaining most traits, showing substantially greater importance than standage, except for tree height and shade tolerance (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}a). Comparisons of the successional signal (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b) reinforced this trend, with strong signals observed only for tree height (Signal = 0.84 on a log scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b) and shade tolerance (Signal = 0.39 on a log scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b). Seed dry mass (Signal = 0.16 on a log scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b) and wood density (Signal = 0.12 on a log scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b) showed lower but noteworthy signals. All other traits had an absolute signal of 0.05 or lower (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b). While most traits increased over time (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b), only bark thickness and leaf nitrogen showed negative trajectories, suggesting marginal decreases with standage (Fig. \ref{fig:fig2}b).

## **Feedback Mechanisms**

Most trait trajectories exhibited approximately linear trends or saturation relationships with standage across feature scenarios, with few directional shifts (supplementary material \ref{fig:S6}). Comparisons of mean intercepts and successional signals between features (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}a) reinforced the strong influence of temperature (mean intercept difference = 0.19 $\pm$ 0.05, mean signal difference = 0.27 $\pm$ 0.04, both on a log scale, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}a) and elevation (mean intercept difference = 0.22 $\pm$ 0.07, mean signal difference = 0.27 $\pm$ 0.06, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}a) observed in the global models. These features heavily filtered traits at the outset of succession and even more strongly modulated successional trajectories, especially under extreme temperature conditions (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b and c). Temperature was a particularly strong filter for stem-related traits (intercept difference for bark thickness = 0.38 and wood density = 0.43, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b) and had moderate filtering effects on other traits (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b). Elevation exerted the strongest filtering on tree height (intercept difference = 0.33, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b), shade tolerance (intercept difference = 0.41, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b), and seed dry mass (intercept difference = 0.53, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b). Temperature and elevation strongly modulated the trajectories of all traits except bark thickness (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}c).\

\noindent Precipitation was the strongest filter across traits (mean intercept difference = 0.39 $\pm$ 0.09, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b), substantially filtering all traits except tree height and seed dry mass, with a particularly strong effect on shade tolerance (intercept difference = 0.85, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b). However, modulation effects were less pronounced, as the successional signal of all traits showed minimal variation between high and low precipitation scenarios (0.12 $\pm$ 0.01, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}c). Soil pH was a stronger filter than the soil’s capacity to retain water (intercept difference: soil pH = 0.26 $\pm$ 0.04, water retention = 0.16 $\pm$ 0.03, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}b) and strongly modulated tree height throughout succession (signal difference = 0.44, Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}c). ater retention of the soil showed no strong modulation effects (mean signal difference = 0.08 $\pm$ 0.01), nor did pH for traits other than height (mean signal difference = 0.11 $\pm$ 0.05).\

```{=tex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=8.43in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/fig3.png}
\caption[Shortened Caption]{Filtering vs. modulation effects of features on trait succession. a. Mean difference}
\label{fig:fig3}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]\ContinuedFloat
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption[]{between intercept and signal of trait trajectories across high and low feature scenarios. b. Differences between high and low feature scenarios per trait in the intercept and c. in the signal. d. Trait positioning in a space of filtering versus modualtion effects.}
\end{figure}
```

\noindent Overall, most traits clustered around intermediate filtering and modulation effects, indicating no clear trade-offs when averaged across conditions for bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, seed dry mass, specific leaf area, and wood density (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}d). Filtering and modulation appeared mutually exclusive only for tree height and shade tolerance: heavily modulated tree height was less sensitive to filtering, while strongly filtered shade tolerance showed little sensitivity to modulation (Fig. \ref{fig:fig3}d).

## **Predictability versus variability across environments**

We did not observe a clear trend in abiotic filtering or modulation regarding predictability. Well-predictable traits generally aligned more with filtering than modulation effects, although modulation was still present. This pattern was evident in bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, specific leaf area, and wood density (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a). In contemporary communities, successional variability is likely driven primarily by abiotic filters, particularly temperature and elevation (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}b), with bark thickness showing the strongest evidence of this (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a). This trend also applied to shade tolerance, albeit with lower predictability (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}). We observed the opposite for tree height, which was generally less predictable. Differences in tree height were more likely induced by modulation with low determinism (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}b), and initial conditions having minimal influence on height trajectories (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a).\

\noindent Overall, successional variability may have different abiotic causes across trait dimensions. Highly deterministic traits may exhibit regional differences due to strong abiotic filters (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}a; see also supplementary material \ref{fig:S7}) and may also show divergence in successional trajectories between high and low temperature and elevation environments. These differences should be predictable and deterministic for traits other than height (Fig. \ref{fig:fig4}b; see also supplementary material \ref{fig:S8}), where biotic dynamics play a greater role. \

\noindent The $\textit{R}^2$ of models excluding stand age left these relationships largely unchanged, with explanatory power decreasing by a mean of 0.02 $\pm$ 0.003, and only four models showing $\textit{R}^2$ reductions between 0.1 and 0.17 (supplementary material \ref{fig:S9}). \


```{=tex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=3.99in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/fig4.png}
\caption{Trait positioning in a space of a. abiotic filtering (abiotic differences in the intercept, i.e., successional outset), and b. abiotic modulation (abiotic differences in the signal, i.e, successional trajectories) against predictability through abiotic features (mean $\textit{R}^2$). Points resemble the mean values per trait and the error bars the standard errors for the respective axes.}
\label{fig:fig4}
\end{figure}
```
\newpage

# DISCUSSION

This work provides the first comprehensive view of functional trait succession across extensive temporal and biogeographic scales. While succession is one of the most thoroughly studied spatio-temporal processes driving forest diversity patterns [@christensen2014], the roles and mechanisms of feedback dynamics between trees and their environment remain poorly understood [@chang2019; @vanbreugelFeedbackLoopsDrive2024]. In this study, we partitioned succession into distinct trait trajectories and found that abiotic conditions, particularly temperature and elevation, play a dominant and predictable role in filtering and modulating the successional trajectories of most traits.\

\noindent Consistent with our first hypothesis, our results show that traits related to stem structure, leaf economics, water transpiration, and reproductive strategies are predominantly shaped by feedbacks to abiotic conditions, limiting functional turnover across succession. In contrast, traits associated with resource competition, such as tree height and shade tolerance, are primarily influenced by changing biotic dynamics, promoting functional turnover. Partly supporting our second hypothesis, we found that temperature, elevation, and precipitation are strong filters that select for functional groups capable of establishing at the outset of succession, with traits overall being more susceptible to filtering than modulation. However, we observed strong modulation effects in environments with varying temperature and elevation across nearly all traits. Contrary to our third hypothesis, abiotic filters significantly contribute to environmental variability in traits, especially those related to stem, leaf, transpiration, and reproduction. Additional variability arises from diverging trait trajectories across temperature and elevation conditions, yet both filtering and modulation appear predictable and deterministic. Traits related to resource competition, however, may introduce less predictable, more site-specific variability in succession, particularly under differing temperature and elevation conditions.\

\noindent The directionality of trait trajectories observed in this study largely aligns with previous research on trait succession. Alongside the expected increases in shade tolerance and tree height, we observed a less pronounced but consistent increase in wood density and seed dry mass. These trends are consistent with earlier findings that link them to the shifting trade-offs between resource acquisition and conservation as succession progresses [@boukili2017; @douma2012; @poorter2008; @wilfahrt2014]. This shift may also explain the slight but consistent decrease in leaf nitrogen, a trend previously associated with reduced photosynthetic performance in old-growth forests [@boukili2017; @lohbeck2014a]. While specific leaf area has been reported to respond to standage in certain biomes [@douma2012; @wilfahrt2014], its effect and directionality were not evident in this study when averaged across environments. The same was true for bark thickness and conduit diameter, traits for which there is limited literature on successional trajectories. \

\noindent Our findings reinforce the established consensus that abiotic conditions play a crucial role in shaping plant diversity patterns [@cornwell2009; @kraft2015a; @suding2008]. Temperature and elevation have long been recognized as critical factors influencing plant diversity [@whittaker1960; @woodward1987; @korner2007], and we demonstrate this to be true at a functional level across extensive temporal and biogeographic scales. Recent research often highlighted temperature as the most significant factor in explaining diversity patterns, both for plants in general [@moles2014] and for trees specifically, also at the functional level [@maynard2022; @paz2024]. We expand on this, showing that temperature and elevation not only filter functional groups but also mediate successional trajectories across different environments. This finding is particularly concerning, as temperature, unlike elevation, is a fluctuating condition undergoing significant changes due to ongoing climate change [@arnell2019]. Therefore, temperature may not only filter trees based on their thermal safety margins [@osullivan2017], but also increase community divergence by influencing functional turnover across trait dimensions. To date, climate warming has been empirically shown to increase the divergence of successional pathways in microbial communities [@guoClimateWarmingLeads2018], but not yet in the more sessile domain of forests. Similar to @guoClimateWarmingLeads2018, we suggest that temperature-induced divergence is predictable in most trait dimensions, except for height and shade tolerance, where stochasticity may be higher. We also found that precipitation, which is similarly affected by climate change [@ranaUnderstandingJointBehavior2017], strongly filters functional traits in early succession but, unlike temperature, shows little effect on modulating trajectories. In contrast, the soil’s capacity to retain water had a negligible effect on most traits and exhibited minimal filtering or modulation capabilities. The role of precipitation in influencing functional diversity has been reported with varying magnitudes [@maynard2022; @moles2014], and its utility as a proxy for water availability has also been questioned [@hickler2009; @moles2014]. In this study, we found that precipitation surpasses the soil’s capacity to retain water in filtering functional groups, but neither factor appears to modulate successional pathways. Consequently, forests may still exhibit divergence if precipitation patterns become more disparate with climate change, but this is likely to occur independently of successional time. \

\noindent Overall, these findings have significant implications for future forest management aimed at meeting conservation goals under different climate scenarios [@borner2020]. As climate conditions shift, the functional profiles of many forests — particularly regarding stem, leaf, transpiration, and seed traits — will undergo a novel and fundamental abiotic selection. This could exacerbate the already observed and increasing global forest die-offs due to heat stress [@anderegg2013]. Moreover, even forest communities that pass these initial filters may face ongoing challenges if temperature shifts drive functional divergence during succession. However, we predict that temperature-induced divergence will be deterministic and predictable, enabling forest management to adapt to climate change scenarios. This underscores the need for further research to establish clear relationships between temperature changes and community divergence, to better prepare for successional shifts induced by climate change.\

\noindent The study of trait succession has made rapid advances in recent years, but several limitations should be acknowledged and addressed in future research. When examining traits across large spatial scales, intraspecific trait variation is often overlooked [@albertIntraspecificFunctionalVariability2010]. In this study, we used species-level trait estimates, which biases results toward a more conservative estimate of trait dynamics but may obscure the potential contribution of intraspecific variation to trait trajectories. A similar limitation applies to ontogenetic variation, i.e., changes in trait expressions as trees progress through different stages of their lifespan [@poorterLeafSizeLeaf2008; @thomasPhotosyntheticDifferencesSaplings2002]. These variations could also contribute to temporal dynamics in trait trajectories or influence successional pathways. Addressing this would require longitudinal studies that include life-stage-specific trait data to provide a more comprehensive view of trait evolution over time [@kunstlerGrowthMortalityMorphological2005]. \

\noindent Although machine learning approaches are increasingly popular in ecology [@christinApplicationsDeepLearning2019] and enable analyses beyond traditional methods, techniques like random forests remain correlative and do not establish causality or parametric relationships [@cutlerRandomForestsClassification2007]. Additionally, they do not account for hierarchical structures, such as the inclusion of space as a random effect. Future modelling approaches within a machine learning framework should therefore incorporate spatial cross-validation to improve generalizability and enhance the robustness of predictions. This is particularly important if the forest data are spatially autocorrelated and not as spatially balanced as the FIA data used in this study. Also, including additional traits could deepen our understanding of trait succession.\

\noindent We emphasized the critical role that tree feedbacks to abiotic conditions play in shaping succession from the onset through its progression. The variability in trait responses to different conditions highlights the complexity of succession and forecasts the vulnerability of contemporary forests to climate change, particularly regenerating forests undergoing successional dynamics. As climate regimes continue to shift, refining these models will be crucial for predicting how forests will respond and for developing adaptive management strategies to mitigate threats and promote the resilience of global forests.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

\
\noindent \textbf{ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS}. My special gratitude is to my supervisor Dr. Daniel S. Maynard who guided and supported me through the entire process of this project. I also thank Alastair Pickering for providing the processed shade tolerance data. Lastly, I thank the University College London for supporting and facilitating this project.\

\noindent \textbf{CODE AND DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT}. The FIA, trait, and environmental data used in this study are openly available in the online supplementary materials of the corresponding publications. All code and the cleaned data used in this study are openly available in the designated [GitHub repository](https://github.com/MerlinWe/succession_traits).\

\noindent \textbf{NOTE FROM THE AUTHOR}. Although this manuscript uses the first person plural to align with common language in scientific publishing, it was solely written by the author mentioned. ChatGPT was used for debugging and improving code efficiency. No AI tool was used to write any code (or text) from scratch.

```{=tex}
\AtEndDocument{
\pagebreak

\section*{SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIAL}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 1-3: Variable descriptives}}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{S\arabic{table}}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Descriptive properties of trait values as they were used in this analyis. Histograms show their respective distribution. Remaining columns show their respecitve mean, minimum, and maximum values, and the unit as used in this analysis (all on a log-scale).}
\includegraphics[width=6.5in, height=8.07in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/tables/trait_descriptives.pdf}
\label{tab:S1}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Descriptive properties of abiotic features as used in this analysis. Histograms show their respective distribution. Remaining columns show their respecitve mean, minimum, and maximum values, and the unit as used in the analysis}
\includegraphics[width=6in, height=4.1in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/tables/feature_descriptives.pdf}
\label{tab:S2}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Biome associations of plots recorded in this study. Table shows count of occurrences per biome.}
\includegraphics[width=5.5in, height=3.1in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/tables/biome_descriptives.pdf}
\label{tab:S3}
\end{table}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 4: Principal Component Analysis}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{figure}{3} 

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.24in, height=4.08in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s4.png}
\caption{Principal component analysis of abiotic variabales. Variables included in the PCA were the sand content of the soil at 15 and 60 cm depth, the water capacity of the soil at 15 and 60 cm depth, annual mean temperature, minimum, maximum, and mean temperatures of the coldest and warmest months, annual precipitation, and precipitation of the wettest, driest, warmest, and coldest quarters. a. Percentage of variance explained by calculated principal components. 3 components showed an eigenvalue greater than 1, i.e., explained a greater amount of variance than a single variable. Together these components explained 83.8 percent of the total variance across abiotic variables included b.- d. Varibale contributions to unique components with an eigenvalue greater than 1 after loadings were rotated using varimax rotation. The first component (b.) showed strong positive association with all temperature varibles. The second component (c.) showed strong positive association with the soil's capacity to retain water, and strong negative association with a higher sand content. The third component (d.) showed strong positive association with all precipitation variables included.}
\label{fig:S4}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 5: Hyperparameter Tuning}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.24in, height=4.08in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s5.png}
\caption{Heatmaps showing the performance of the hyperparameters ‘number of trees’ (the facets), ‘minimum node size’, and ‘number of variables included at each splitting point (mtry)’ in model tuning. Displayed is the prediction error for random forest models across the eight traits included: wood density, bark thickness, conduit diameter, leaf nitrogen, specific leaf area, seed dry mass, shade tolerance, and tree height. Each subplot corresponds to a specific number of trees (500, 1000, 1500), with the x-axis representing mtry and the y-axis representing the minimum node size. The color of each tile reflects the prediction error, with darker colors indicating lower errors and thus better model performance. The patterns across the heatmaps reveal optimal hyperparameter combinations for each trait, helping to identify the tuning that minimize prediction error and enhance model accuracy.}
\label{fig:S5}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 6: Partial Dependence Curves of Feature Scenarios}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=5.33in, height=7.36in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s6.png}
\captionof{figure}{Partial dependence curves of traits in all high and low environmental scenarios (a.–e.) and their predictive power (f.). Curves are smoothed by fitting a Generalized Additive Model (GAM) with cubic spline smoothing.}
\label{fig:S6}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 7: Intercept Differences per Trait and per Feature}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=5.02in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s7.png}
\captionof{figure}{Trait positioning in a space of abiotic filtering (abiotic differences in the intercept, i.e., successional outset) against predictability through abiotic features (mean $\textit{R}^2$ of both scenarios) for every feature. Points resemble the trait values.}
\label{fig:S7}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 8: Signal Differences per Trait and per Feature}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=5.02in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s8.png}
\captionof{figure}{Trait positioning in a space of abiotic modulation (abiotic differences in the signal, i.e., successional trajectory) against predictability through abiotic features (mean $\textit{R}^2$ of both scenarios) for every feature. Points resemble the trait values.}
\label{fig:S8}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\textbf{Supplementary Material 9: Difference in scenario model accuracy with standage exclusion}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=6.27in, height=5.13in]{/Users/serpent/Documents/MSc/Thesis/Code/output/plots/supplementary/s9.png}
\captionof{figure}{Changes in the $\textit{R}^2$ of scenario models if standage was not included as a predictive feature. If standage is excluded, the $\textit{R}^2$ resembles the true environmental predictability by only abiotic features.}
\label{fig:S9}
\end{figure}

}
```
